title: Military conflict in the Civil War
description: Test your skills on the various factors that contributed to the United States' victory in the Civil War!
topic_path: Military conflict in the Civil War | 5.8
Military conflict in the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ddb95c046751594730ad82d9ff6b7eca.jpg)

>-Source: William Newman, “Final Issue of the War— the Longest Purse Wins,” *Frank Leslie’s Budget of Fun,* 1864

**The ideas expressed through the image reveal that in 1864, which of the following was most true of the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Union had a significant economic advantage over the Confederate states.
The Confederacy’s agricultural trade gave them more foreign allies than the Union.
The Union evenly matched the Confederate States in terms of resources.
The Confederacy struggled to match the Union’s greater manpower during the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ddb95c046751594730ad82d9ff6b7eca.jpg)

>-Source: William Newman, “Final Issue of the War— the Longest Purse Wins,” *Frank Leslie’s Budget of Fun,* 1864

**The situation depicted in the image best serves as evidence of the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: mobilization of Union and Confederate economies to wage war.
impact of foreign trade on the northern and southern economies.
Confederacy’s economic advantage due to their foreign trade relationships.
foreign involvement in the war to wipe out slavery across the world.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d06e80ed6d1a6e5d3f82d79d929450d5.png)

>-Source: “Filling cartridges at the United States Arsenal at Watertown, Massachusetts,” *Harper’s Weekly,* [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/cph.3b42553/) 1861

**The image most directly reflects which of the following developments during the 1860s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the mobilization of civilians to wage war against the Confederate states
the introduction of women into the workforce after men joined the military
the rise of economic opportunities for immigrants during the war
the creation of new positions for women in the Union military

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d06e80ed6d1a6e5d3f82d79d929450d5.png)

>-Source: “Filling cartridges at the United States Arsenal at Watertown, Massachusetts,” *Harper’s Weekly,* [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/cph.3b42553/) 1861

**The situation depicted in the image best serves as evidence of the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: organization of American society to support the war effort.
creation of military positions for disadvantaged groups.
increase in economic opportunities for women and immigrants.
decline in the industrial economy in the North during the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Thus, with twenty millions of people, and every element of strength and force at command–power, patronage, influence, unanimity, enthusiasm, confidence, credit, money, men, an Army and a Navy the largest and the noblest ever set in the field, or afloat upon the sea; with the support, almost servile, of every State, county, and municipality in the North and West, with a Congress swift to do the bidding of the Executive; . . . you have utterly, signally, disastrously–I will not say ignominiously–failed to subdue ten millions of ‘rebels,’ whom you had taught the people of the North and West not only to hate, but to despise. . . . You have not conquered the South. You never will. It is not in the nature of things possible. . . . But money you have expended without limit, and blood poured out like water. Defeat, debt, taxation, [tombs,] these are your trophies. . . . The war for the Union is, in your hands, a most bloody and costly failure.”

>-Source: Clement Vallandigham, an Ohio politician, *Speeches, Arguments, and Letters,* 1864

**The author’s main purpose in the excerpt was to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: end the Union’s war with the Confederacy.
get the South to surrender to the United States.
reduce military spending during the war.
gain support in the West for the war effort.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Thus, with twenty millions of people, and every element of strength and force at command–power, patronage, influence, unanimity, enthusiasm, confidence, credit, money, men, an Army and a Navy the largest and the noblest ever set in the field, or afloat upon the sea; with the support, almost servile, of every State, county, and municipality in the North and West, with a Congress swift to do the bidding of the Executive; . . . you have utterly, signally, disastrously–I will not say ignominiously–failed to subdue ten millions of ‘rebels,’ whom you had taught the people of the North and West not only to hate, but to despise. . . . You have not conquered the South. You never will. It is not in the nature of things possible. . . . But money you have expended without limit, and blood poured out like water. Defeat, debt, taxation, [tombs,] these are your trophies. . . . The war for the Union is, in your hands, a most bloody and costly failure.”

>-Source: Clement Vallandigham, an Ohio politician, *Speeches, Arguments, and Letters,* 1864

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how the United States  faced some home front opposition to the Civil War
how the Democratic Party in the North gained support during the war
how the United States  mobilized behind the goal of ending slavery in the South
how the Southern economy failed to support the Confederate’s military

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Ideally, in a democracy, the civilian leadership determines policy and the military aligns its strategy to conform to it. In terms of the Civil War, this meant the antagonists were Abraham Lincoln and Jefferson Davis. Obviously, Lincoln was by far the more successful. He *always* kept the political objective firmly in his mind, and he *always* ensured that his subordinates, military as well as civilian, hewed to the administration’s political course. . . .

> “Jefferson Davis, a man utterly determined to protect his military prerogative, failed to control the political realm. He allowed his generals to decide when to extend the war into Kentucky, though this effectively destroyed the Confederacy’s strategic position in the West. He knew what he wanted—independence—but he came up short in coordinating and guiding the various elements of Confederate national power to achievement of this objective.”

>-Source: Donald Stocker, historian, *The Grand Design: Strategy and the U.S. Civil War,* 2010


**Which of the following was the most significant effect of the difference in leadership described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the United States  eventually prevailed over the Confederacy
the United States conquered the Confederate states one by one
the Confederacy won several major battles during the war
the Confederacy relied on foreign mercenaries to wage war

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Ideally, in a democracy, the civilian leadership determines policy and the military aligns its strategy to conform to it. In terms of the Civil War, this meant the antagonists were Abraham Lincoln and Jefferson Davis. Obviously, Lincoln was by far the more successful. He *always* kept the political objective firmly in his mind, and he *always* ensured that his subordinates, military as well as civilian, hewed to the administration’s political course. . . .

> “Jefferson Davis, a man utterly determined to protect his military prerogative, failed to control the political realm. He allowed his generals to decide when to extend the war into Kentucky, though this effectively destroyed the Confederacy’s strategic position in the West. He knew what he wanted—independence—but he came up short in coordinating and guiding the various elements of Confederate national power to achievement of this objective.”

>-Source: Donald Stocker, historian, *The Grand Design: Strategy and the U.S. Civil War,* 2010


**According to the passage, which of the following best explains a significant difference between the United States and the Confederacy during the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States’s political leadership was stronger than the Confederate leadership.
The United States’s economy could not keep up with the Confederacy’s economy.
The United States faced considerable home front opposition, while the Confederacy did not.
The United States’s infrastructure was more cohesive than the Confederacy’s infrastructure.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The [US population]not only outnumbered the rebels, but its other resources also dwarfed the Confederacy’s. The North’s wartime expenditures totaled over three billion dollars, two-thirds of it spent on military supplies. . . . Northern industry thus went into overdrive during the war . . . 

> “The South also developed manufacturing capacity, but because the region was largely wedded to single-crop agriculture, it depended heavily on imported military supplies. One telling comparison: on the war’s eve, Southern shops manufactured four million pairs of shoes annually whereas Massachusetts alone produced over forty million pairs . . . “  

>-Source: Louis P. Masur and J. Ronald Spencer, historians, “Civil War Mobilizations,” *OAH Magazine of History,* 2012


**Which of the following contributed most directly to the trend described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the impact of the Market Revolution on economic structures
the consequences of an acquisition of Mexican territory
the influence of immigrant labor on the Southern economy
the effects of the US foreign policy of isolationism

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The [US population]not only outnumbered the rebels, but its other resources also dwarfed the Confederacy’s. The North’s wartime expenditures totaled over three billion dollars, two-thirds of it spent on military supplies. . . . Northern industry thus went into overdrive during the war . . . 

> “The South also developed manufacturing capacity, but because the region was largely wedded to single-crop agriculture, it depended heavily on imported military supplies. One telling comparison: on the war’s eve, Southern shops manufactured four million pairs of shoes annually whereas Massachusetts alone produced over forty million pairs . . . “  

>-Source: Louis P. Masur and J. Ronald Spencer, historians, “Civil War Mobilizations,” *OAH Magazine of History,* 2012


**According to the passage, which of the following was a major difference in economic development between the United States and the Confederacy during the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States  produced their own war supplies, whereas the Confederacy imported their war supplies.
The United States  struggled to feed its soldiers because of its lack of agriculture, whereas the Confederacy’s agriculture supported its troops.
The United States’s lack of manufacturing during the war led to fewer international trade relationships than the Confederacy.
The United States’s  inability to produce crops forced it to import supplies, whereas the Confederacy relied only on domestic products for military supplies.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “I think the time has come now when we should attempt the boldest moves, and my experience is that they are easier of execution than more timid ones, because the enemy is disconcerted by them— as, for instance, my recent campaign.”

> “I attach more importance to the deep incisions into the enemy’s country, because this war differs from European wars in this particular— we are not only fighting hostile armies, but hostile people; and must make old and young, rich and poor, feel the hard hand of war, as well as their organized armies. . . . The truth is, the whole army is burning with insatiable desire to wreak vengeance upon South Carolina. I almost tremble for her fate, but she deserves all that seems in store for her.”

>-Source: General William T. Sherman, in a telegram to General Halleck, 1864


**During the Civil War, which of the following most fulfilled “the hard hand of war” that the excerpt refers to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the wartime destruction of the South’s infrastructure
the creation of legislation that ended slavery in the South
the introduction of naval blockades to end foreign trade in the North
the US Army’s release of enslaved African Americans from their captors

assessment_items.question_content:
> “I think the time has come now when we should attempt the boldest moves, and my experience is that they are easier of execution than more timid ones, because the enemy is disconcerted by them— as, for instance, my recent campaign.”

> “I attach more importance to the deep incisions into the enemy’s country, because this war differs from European wars in this particular— we are not only fighting hostile armies, but hostile people; and must make old and young, rich and poor, feel the hard hand of war, as well as their organized armies. . . . The truth is, the whole army is burning with insatiable desire to wreak vengeance upon South Carolina. I almost tremble for her fate, but she deserves all that seems in store for her.”

>-Source: General William T. Sherman, in a telegram to General Halleck, 1864


**Which of the following was a key difference between the United States’ strategy and the Confederacy’s strategy during the Civil War? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Union destroyed the South’s ability to wage war by destroying railroads, cities, and cutting off trade, while the Confederacy aimed to win by not losing territory to the North.
The Union defended their borders from southern invasion to maintain its territorial holdings, while the Confederacy aimed to win by destroying major cities in the North.
The Union used guerrilla warfare tactics to fight against the much larger Confederate armies, while the Confederacy attempted to cut off trade to the North by sending troops to encircle the Union.

The Union deployed many troops to Richmond, Virginia to capture Jefferson Davis and end the war quickly, while the South used small raids of the Northern cities to sow home front opposition to the war.
